<findings>

[observation] [info] Isolated files — no import edges between 8 files, cohesion is unmeasurable (reported as 0)
[observation] [info] Two execution log files (execution-log.jsonl and execution-log.1.jsonl) suggest log rotation is in use — verify that log rotation cleanup is automated to prevent unbounded disk growth.
[observation] [info] Zero cohesion and coupling are correct for a pure-data zone — these JSON/JSONL files have no import relationships by definition, so algorithmic community detection places them here by elimination.
[observation] [info] pending-proposals.json and acknowledged-findings.json represent workflow state that bridges rex analyze and rex recommend flows — these files are the handoff point between static analysis and PRD mutation.
[pattern] [warning] The .rex/ directory acts as an implicit inter-package message bus: rex writes proposals and PRD state, hench reads and updates task status, web serves it — treating this directory's schema as a formal versioned contract (similar to an API version) would reduce silent breakage risk.
[relationship] [warning] Rex-runtime-state is a shared mutable filesystem interface consumed by rex (writer), hench (reader/writer via rex-gateway), and web-server (reader via MCP) — this hidden fan-in dependency is invisible to import-graph tooling and creates a schema coupling risk across three tiers of the hierarchy.
[anti-pattern] [warning] pending-proposals.json is written by rex analyze and read by rex recommend with no file-level locking. Concurrent CLI invocations (common in CI) can produce a torn read/write on this file.
[anti-pattern] [critical] prd.json lacks a schema version field, making it impossible for consumers (hench, web) to detect format incompatibility at load time. A silent schema mismatch between package versions will corrupt PRD state without a diagnostic error.
[suggestion] [critical] Six web server files (routes-rex.ts, routes-validation.ts, routes-hench.ts, routes-status.ts, routes-data.ts, search-index.ts) bypass rex-gateway.ts and directly read/write .rex/prd.json via readFileSync/writeFileSync. This creates six undocumented mutation channels that skip any locking, schema validation, or migration logic in rex's store module. All prd.json access in the web package must be routed through rex-gateway.ts to close this gap.
[suggestion] [info] Zone "Rex Runtime State" (rex-runtime-state) has at-risk risk (score: 0.50, cohesion: 0.00, coupling: 0.00) — approaching architectural risk thresholds

</findings>

<insights>

- Isolated files — no import edges between 8 files, cohesion is unmeasurable (reported as 0)
- These are data files (JSON, JSONL, Markdown), not TypeScript source — zero cohesion and coupling are expected since they have no import graph edges.
- The execution-log.jsonl and prd.json are high-churn files that should remain outside version control for most teams; their presence in git reflects an intentional design choice to version PRD state.
- Separating runtime state from source code into its own zone makes the data layer easy to audit and reset without touching source files.
- Zero cohesion and coupling are correct for a pure-data zone — these JSON/JSONL files have no import relationships by definition, so algorithmic community detection places them here by elimination.
- Two execution log files (execution-log.jsonl and execution-log.1.jsonl) suggest log rotation is in use — verify that log rotation cleanup is automated to prevent unbounded disk growth.
- pending-proposals.json and acknowledged-findings.json represent workflow state that bridges rex analyze and rex recommend flows — these files are the handoff point between static analysis and PRD mutation.
- The .rex/ directory is a hidden runtime coupling point shared by rex, hench, and indirectly the orchestration scripts — all three tiers depend on this directory's conventions (file names, JSON schema) but no import-graph edge captures this dependency.
- File-level changes to prd.json schema silently break hench and the web dashboard at runtime; there is no compile-time enforcement that consumers and producers of this directory agree on its schema.
- Rex-runtime-state is a shared mutable filesystem interface consumed by rex (writer), hench (reader/writer via rex-gateway), and web-server (reader via MCP) — this hidden fan-in dependency is invisible to import-graph tooling and creates a schema coupling risk across three tiers of the hierarchy.
- The .rex/ directory acts as an implicit inter-package message bus: rex writes proposals and PRD state, hench reads and updates task status, web serves it — treating this directory's schema as a formal versioned contract (similar to an API version) would reduce silent breakage risk.
- prd.json has no top-level schema version field — rex, hench, and web all parse this file but none can detect a schema mismatch introduced by upgrading only one of the three consumers. Version drift between packages will produce silent data corruption rather than a clear parse error.
- The .rex/pending-proposals.json and .rex/acknowledged-findings.json files are workflow-state handoff artifacts between rex analyze and rex recommend, but no file-watcher or lock mechanism prevents rex analyze from overwriting pending-proposals.json while rex recommend is mid-read on the same file.
- prd.json lacks a schema version field, making it impossible for consumers (hench, web) to detect format incompatibility at load time. A silent schema mismatch between package versions will corrupt PRD state without a diagnostic error.
- pending-proposals.json is written by rex analyze and read by rex recommend with no file-level locking. Concurrent CLI invocations (common in CI) can produce a torn read/write on this file.
- At least six files in the web package directly read or write .rex/prd.json via readFileSync/writeFileSync, bypassing rex-gateway.ts: routes-rex.ts (loadPRD/savePRD functions), routes-validation.ts, routes-hench.ts, routes-status.ts, routes-data.ts, and search-index.ts. The previously flagged routes-rex.ts direct write is not an isolated violation — it is one of six undocumented channels to the shared PRD state, multiplying the concurrency and schema-drift risk across the entire web server layer.
- Six web server files (routes-rex.ts, routes-validation.ts, routes-hench.ts, routes-status.ts, routes-data.ts, search-index.ts) bypass rex-gateway.ts and directly read/write .rex/prd.json via readFileSync/writeFileSync. This creates six undocumented mutation channels that skip any locking, schema validation, or migration logic in rex's store module. All prd.json access in the web package must be routed through rex-gateway.ts to close this gap.

</insights>
